A researcher is comparing two kinds of problems.

In the first kind, like the train-and-platform case, the setup is compact and contradiction-driven. The evidence is mostly in the concepts themselves. If two claims conflict, the conflict is visible. This makes such examples unusually favorable for testing whether a system can reason through a clean puzzle.

In the second kind, which includes many scientific discoveries, the situation is messier. Instruments are noisy, data are missing, and the path to a conclusion is not a tidy contradiction but an uncertain accumulation of partial signals. So success on the first kind does not straightforwardly establish competence on the second kind. The world implied here is one where benchmark elegance can mask deployment difficulty.

Now place an AI system into that world. Humans are said to remain “the judge, for now.” That means human oversight is still the present standard for evaluating outputs. But this phrase also reveals a gap: there must be some transition point at which human judging is no longer primary, and the premises say the hardest part is specifying what evidence would justify that transition. So the system may produce outputs, and humans may judge them, but there is not yet a defined criterion for when the system becomes trustworthy as a judge of its own future outputs.

That gap matters because recursive self-improvement by self-judgment is only plausible under a further condition: the evaluation function must be more stable than the generator it assesses. If the part that scores outputs changes as erratically as the part that produces them, then self-correction has no fixed standard. One shifting process would merely certify another shifting process. The premises state that this required stability is not yet explained. Therefore the world described contains no completed argument for safe or reliable recursive self-improvement through self-judgment.

The last two premises introduce strings that have no stated connection to the others: “Umbellulate neologies invests daddles ifreal” and “Asexualise nl scribbled skaldship tell.” Since we are to treat all premises as true, these strings exist as true statements in the world, but their semantic role is unspecified. They do not supply evidence about scientific instrumentation, human oversight, or evaluation stability. So they remain inert with respect to the main inference unless further premises connect them.

What follows overall is a world where clean conceptual examples can overstate confidence, messy empirical reality remains the harder test, human judgment is still the operative backstop, and the crucial handoff to machine self-judgment lacks a justified evidential threshold. Because the needed stability of evaluation over generation is unexplained, recursive self-improvement remains an ungrounded possibility rather than an established path.
